content.text: App Object vs Service Principal

In Microsoft Entra ID, an application's identity is split across two distinct object types: the App Registration (App Object) and the Service Principal. Understanding the difference between them is fundamental to configuring authentication, permissions, and multi-tenant access correctly. They serve different purposes and exist in different places within the Entra ID directory structure.

attrs.alt: App Registration (single, home tenant) instantiates Service Principals in each tenant where the app is consented

content.text: The App Registration (Application Object)

The App Registration is a global blueprint that defines an application's identity. It exists in exactly one Entra ID tenant — the tenant where the developer registered the application (called the home tenant). It is also referred to as the Application Object.
Exists in exactly one tenant — the home tenant.
Has a stable Application ID (client ID / appId) that is the same across all tenants.
Owns the application's credentials: client secrets and certificates.
Defines the API permissions the application requests (Microsoft Graph, custom APIs).
Configures authentication settings: redirect URIs, token version, supported account types.
Controls which tenants can use the app (single-tenant vs multi-tenant).
Contains the App Roles and OAuth 2.0 scopes the application exposes to other apps.
Creating an App Registration

# Create an app registration in the home tenant
az ad app create \
    --display-name 'My Backend Service' \
    --sign-in-audience AzureADMyOrg  # single-tenant

# Multi-tenant (users from any Entra ID tenant can sign in)
az ad app create \
    --display-name 'My SaaS App' \
    --sign-in-audience AzureADMultipleOrgs

# View the resulting appId (client ID) — same across all tenants
az ad app list --display-name 'My Backend Service' \
    --query '[0].{AppId:appId, ObjectId:id, DisplayName:displayName}'
The Service Principal

A Service Principal is a local instance of an application within a specific tenant. While the App Registration is the global blueprint, the Service Principal is the per-tenant identity the application uses when it operates in that tenant. It holds local configuration: role assignments, admin consent records, and sign-in logs for that tenant.
One Service Principal per tenant where the app is installed or consented.
Has an Object ID (objectId) that is unique per tenant — different in every tenant.
Shares the same Application ID (appId) as the App Registration.
Holds RBAC role assignments granted within that tenant.
Records admin consent decisions for API permissions in that tenant.
Sign-in and audit logs are associated with the Service Principal, not the App Object.
Can be enabled or disabled independently per tenant.
Listing Service Principals

# List all service principals — includes managed identities, enterprise apps, first-party services
az ad sp list --all --query '[].{AppId:appId, ObjectId:id, DisplayName:displayName, Type:servicePrincipalType}' --output table

# Find the service principal for a specific app registration
APP_ID='aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff'
az ad sp show --id "$APP_ID" --query '{ObjectId:id, AppId:appId, DisplayName:displayName}'

# Service principal types:
# Application   — from an app registration (yours or third-party)
# ManagedIdentity — system- or user-assigned managed identity
# Legacy          — older format, not recommended for new apps
Key Identifiers
Identifier
Where it Lives
Stability
Use
Application ID (appId / client_id)
App Registration + all Service Principals
Same across all tenants, never changes
OAuth 2.0 client_id in auth flows; IMDS client_id param
Object ID (id / objectId) — App
App Registration only (home tenant)
Stable; unique to the App Object
Manage the app registration via Graph API
Object ID (id / objectId) — SP
Service Principal (per-tenant)
Stable within a tenant; different across tenants
RBAC role assignments; Graph API calls targeting this tenant
Principal ID
Service Principal
Same as SP Object ID
Used in role assignments and audit logs
Tenant ID (tid)
Entra ID tenant
Stable per tenant
Identifies which tenant issued a token

When assigning Azure RBAC roles, always use the Service Principal's Object ID (principal ID), not the Application ID. They look similar (both are GUIDs) but are different values with different purposes.
How Service Principals Are Created
A Service Principal in a tenant is created by one of these events:
App Registration creation — Entra ID automatically creates a Service Principal in the home tenant.
Admin consent — a tenant admin consents to a multi-tenant app, creating a Service Principal in their tenant.
User consent — if admin consent is not required, the first user to sign in creates the Service Principal.
az ad sp create — explicit creation via CLI, pointing at an existing App Registration by its appId.
Managed identity provisioning — Azure creates an App Registration and Service Principal automatically when you enable a managed identity on a resource.

# Explicitly create a service principal for an existing app registration
# (useful in multi-tenant scenarios or CI/CD setup)
APP_ID='aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff'
az ad sp create --id "$APP_ID"

# Create a service principal with a new app registration in one step (common for CI/CD)
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
    --name 'github-actions-deployer' \
    --role Contributor \
    --scopes /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/MyRG \
    --json-auth  # outputs credential JSON for GitHub Actions AZURE_CREDENTIALS secret
Single-Tenant vs Multi-Tenant Apps

The App Registration's signInAudience setting determines which tenants can use the app. Single-tenant apps have a Service Principal only in the home tenant. Multi-tenant apps can have a Service Principal in any Entra ID tenant whose users or admins consent to the app.
signInAudience Value
Who Can Sign In
Service Principal Exists In
AzureADMyOrg
Users from the home tenant only
Home tenant only
AzureADMultipleOrgs
Users from any Entra ID organizational tenant
Any tenant where consent is given
AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount
Entra ID users + personal Microsoft accounts
Any Entra ID tenant + MSA realm
PersonalMicrosoftAccount
Personal Microsoft accounts only
MSA realm only
Querying via Microsoft Graph

The Microsoft Graph API exposes both object types under separate endpoints. The /applications endpoint returns App Registrations; the /servicePrincipals endpoint returns Service Principals. The appId field links them.

# Get app registration by display name
az rest --method GET \
  --uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications?$filter=displayName eq \'My Backend Service\'&$select=id,appId,displayName' \
  --query 'value[0]'

# Get service principal for the same app
az rest --method GET \
  --uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals?$filter=appId eq \'aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff\'&$select=id,appId,displayName,servicePrincipalType' \
  --query 'value[0]'

# Note: /applications returns objects with 'id' = App Object ID
#       /servicePrincipals returns objects with 'id' = SP Object ID
# Both share the same 'appId'
Python — Microsoft Graph SDK

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from msgraph import GraphServiceClient
from msgraph.generated.service_principals.service_principals_request_builder import \
    ServicePrincipalsRequestBuilder
from kiota_abstractions.base_request_configuration import RequestConfiguration

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
graph_client = GraphServiceClient(credential)

async def get_sp_by_app_id(app_id: str):
    query = ServicePrincipalsRequestBuilder.ServicePrincipalsRequestBuilderGetQueryParameters(
        filter=f"appId eq '{app_id}'",
        select=['id', 'appId', 'displayName', 'servicePrincipalType']
    )
    config = RequestConfiguration(query_parameters=query)
    result = await graph_client.service_principals.get(request_configuration=config)
    return result.value[0] if result.value else None
Permissions: App Registration vs Service Principal

API permission requests are defined on the App Registration. But consent (whether those permissions are actually granted) is recorded on the Service Principal in each tenant. This distinction matters when troubleshooting access: a permission can be defined in the App Registration but not consented in a specific tenant's Service Principal.

# View delegated permissions requested in the App Registration
APP_OBJ_ID='<app-registration-object-id>'
az ad app show --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --query 'requiredResourceAccess[].{API:resourceAppId, Permissions:resourceAccess[].id}'

# View consented permissions on the Service Principal (what's actually granted)
SP_OBJ_ID='<service-principal-object-id>'
az rest --method GET \
    --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$SP_OBJ_ID/oauth2PermissionGrants" \
    --query 'value[].{Scope:scope, ConsentType:consentType}'

# Grant admin consent for all configured permissions (tenant-wide)
az ad app permission admin-consent --id "$APP_OBJ_ID"
Disabling a Service Principal

You can disable the Service Principal in a specific tenant without deleting the App Registration or affecting the app in other tenants. This effectively blocks all sign-ins and token issuance for the app in that tenant.

# Disable a service principal (blocks sign-ins in this tenant only)
SP_OBJ_ID='<service-principal-object-id>'
az ad sp update --id "$SP_OBJ_ID" --set accountEnabled=false

# Re-enable
az ad sp update --id "$SP_OBJ_ID" --set accountEnabled=true

# Verify
az ad sp show --id "$SP_OBJ_ID" --query '{Enabled:accountEnabled, DisplayName:displayName}'
App Roles — Defined in App Registration, Assigned on Service Principal

App Roles are custom authorization roles defined in the App Registration manifest. When users or service principals are assigned these roles, the assignment is stored on the Service Principal. The role claims appear in the access token issued when the service principal authenticates.
Define App Roles in the App Registration
Assign App Role to a Service Principal (Application Permission)

# Assign the 'Data.Ingest' app role to a calling service principal
# (service-to-service, no user context)

RESOURCE_SP_ID='<target-app-service-principal-object-id>'
CALLER_SP_ID='<calling-service-principal-object-id>'
APP_ROLE_ID='aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-222222222222'

az rest --method POST \
    --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$RESOURCE_SP_ID/appRoleAssignedTo" \
    --body "{
        \"principalId\": \"$CALLER_SP_ID\",
        \"resourceId\": \"$RESOURCE_SP_ID\",
        \"appRoleId\": \"$APP_ROLE_ID\"
    }"

# Verify the assignment
az rest --method GET \
    --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$RESOURCE_SP_ID/appRoleAssignedTo" \
    --query 'value[].{Principal:principalDisplayName, Role:appRoleId}'
Token Claims with App Roles
Credentials: Stored on App Registration, Used by Service Principal

Client secrets and certificates are configured on the App Registration, not on the Service Principal. However, the Service Principal in each tenant uses those credentials to authenticate. A credential expiry on the App Registration affects all tenants simultaneously.

# Add a client secret to the App Registration
APP_OBJ_ID='<app-registration-object-id>'

SECRET=$(az ad app credential reset \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --append \
    --display-name 'prod-secret-2025' \
    --years 1 \
    --query '{ClientSecret:password, AppId:appId, TenantId:tenant}')
echo "$SECRET"

# Add a certificate credential (more secure than secrets)
az ad app credential reset \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --cert '@/path/to/public-cert.pem' \
    --append \
    --display-name 'prod-cert-2025'

# List all credentials and their expiry dates
az ad app credential list --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --query '[].{Name:displayName, Type:type, Expiry:endDateTime}'

Prefer certificate credentials over client secrets. Certificates use asymmetric cryptography — the private key never leaves your environment, so there is no secret to leak. Secrets are symmetric: whoever has the string can authenticate as your app.
KQL: Audit App Registration and Service Principal Changes

// Track changes to app registrations and service principals
// Covers credential additions, permission grants, and role assignments
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where Category in ('ApplicationManagement', 'EntitlementManagement')
| where OperationName in (
    'Add application',
    'Delete application',
    'Update application',
    'Add service principal',
    'Delete service principal',
    'Add app role assignment to service principal',
    'Add delegated permission grant',
    'Update application -- Certificates and secrets management'
  )
| extend
    AppName = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName),
    Actor   = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName),
    Changes = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties)
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, AppName, Actor, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Alert: new credential added to any app registration in the last 24h
// Catches unauthorized secret/certificate additions
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where OperationName == 'Update application -- Certificates and secrets management'
| extend AppName = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| extend Actor   = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| extend CredOp  = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties[0].newValue)
| where CredOp has 'KeyCredentials' or CredOp has 'PasswordCredentials'
| project TimeGenerated, AppName, Actor, CredOp, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Managed Identities — Under the Hood

When you enable a managed identity, Azure performs the same App Registration + Service Principal creation that you do manually for a regular app, but does so automatically and ties the credentials to the hardware of the host compute resource. The resulting Service Principal is visible in Entra ID and behaves identically to a regular service principal for RBAC and audit purposes.

# After enabling a system-assigned identity on a VM, inspect its service principal
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm identity show --resource-group RG --name MyVM --query principalId -o tsv)

# The principal ID is the SP object ID
az ad sp show --id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --query '{DisplayName:displayName, Type:servicePrincipalType, AppId:appId}'

# servicePrincipalType will be 'ManagedIdentity'
# displayName matches the Azure resource name (for system-assigned)

# User-assigned identity: has its own App Registration + SP
az identity show --resource-group RG --name my-identity \
    --query '{ClientId:clientId, PrincipalId:principalId, TenantId:tenantId}'

# clientId = appId (the Application ID)
# principalId = SP object ID (use for RBAC assignments)
Common Confusion Points
Confusion
Correct Understanding
'The appId and objectId are the same'
They are different GUIDs. appId (client ID) is constant across tenants. objectId differs per App Object vs per SP.
'I can assign RBAC with the appId'
No — RBAC assignments require the SP's objectId (principal ID). Using appId fails silently or targets the wrong object.
'Deleting the app registration removes the SP everywhere'
Yes — deleting the App Registration removes the SP in the home tenant AND in all other tenants (they lose the app).
'Disabling the SP in one tenant affects other tenants'
No — the SP is per-tenant. Disabling it only blocks sign-ins in that tenant.
'Managed identity SPs have an App Registration'
Partially: user-assigned identities have a lightweight App Registration. System-assigned identities do not expose one in the portal but still have one under the hood.
Summary

The App Registration (Application Object) is a single global blueprint that lives in the home tenant and owns credentials, permission requests, and authentication configuration. The Service Principal is a per-tenant instance — every tenant where the app is used gets its own Service Principal with its own Object ID. Both share the same Application ID (appId / client_id). Managed identities also create a Service Principal (without a separate App Registration). When assigning RBAC roles or troubleshooting consent, use the Service Principal's Object ID, not the Application ID. Disabling a Service Principal blocks sign-ins in that tenant without affecting the app elsewhere.
